behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode spans two modules — the validator (which must detect missing fields) and the DLQ writer (which must record the original row number). A unit test on the validator alone would not catch a bug where the row number is dropped before being handed to the DLQ writer. The behavior explicitly requires verifying what ends up in the DLQ, which means wiring validator output through the DLQ routing layer. MOCK-HIDE: The DLQ can be a local fake/stub — we don't need a real message queue to catch this failure. Mocking the DLQ sink and asserting on what was sent is sufficient. No real I/O, LLM, or multi-step agentic logic is required.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a small CSV in-memory with rows that have missing required fields alongside valid rows. Run the pipeline's validation-and-routing layer with a fake DLQ sink. Assert that: (a) invalid rows were not forwarded to the success path, (b) the DLQ sink received exactly the invalid rows, and (c) each DLQ entry carries the correct original row number (1-indexed or 0-indexed per spec). Use parametrized cases: missing one field, missing multiple fields, field present but empty.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is a pure algorithmic/parsing function. The failure mode is entirely within the transformation logic itself — incorrect regex, wrong strptime pattern, ambiguous date resolution (e.g., 04/05/06). There is no module boundary that must be crossed for this bug to manifest. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: No I/O, no external dependency, and nothing to mock. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. Parametrized unit tests with representative date strings are both necessary and sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Write a parametrized unit test over the date-transformation function with at least: ISO 8601 (2024-03-15), US format (03/15/2024), European format (15.03.2024 and 15/03/2024), two-digit years, and boundary ambiguities (e.g., 01/02/03). Assert each input produces the expected canonical output (e.g., 2024-03-15). Also test that an unrecognized format raises the expected exception rather than silently producing a wrong date.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Memory usage and wall-clock latency are OS-level properties. A unit or integration test against in-memory data structures does not exercise the actual streaming/buffering path the way a real large file does — streaming parsers, chunk sizes, GC pressure, and I/O scheduling all interact at the OS level. MOCK-HIDE: If you mock S3 with a local large file, the processing code itself still needs to run against 500K real rows to surface memory growth or timeout. A small synthetic dataset would always pass. A real subprocess (or at minimum a large local file) is needed so that peak RSS and elapsed time can be measured accurately against the configured limits.
behaviors.test_description: Generate a 500,000-row CSV file on local disk (or stream from a mock S3 endpoint). Launch the pipeline as a subprocess (or call it with tracemalloc and a real timer). Assert that: (a) elapsed wall time stays within the configured timeout, (b) peak memory (RSS or tracemalloc peak) stays below an acceptable ceiling. Run once; flakiness from load spikes is acceptable here — a consistent OOM or timeout is the signal. If the pipeline configuration exposes a timeout value, set it to a known threshold and verify the pipeline completes before it fires.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode sits at the boundary between the S3 client and the retry/backoff wrapper — not inside either one alone. A unit test of the retry policy with a dummy callable would not catch a bug where the S3 client swallows the 503 before the retry wrapper sees it, or where the error type returned by the S3 SDK doesn't match what the retry logic checks. Wiring a mock S3 client that returns 503 on the first N calls through the actual retry layer is required. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: A locally controlled fake S3 endpoint (or boto3 stub) that injects 503 responses is sufficient — a real AWS call is not needed, and would introduce nondeterminism. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: No.
behaviors.test_description: Use boto3's Stubber (or an equivalent HTTP-level mock) to inject one or two 503 responses followed by a 200. Run the pipeline's S3-fetch layer against this stubbed client. Assert that: (a) the pipeline does not raise or abort on the 503, (b) the fetch is retried the expected number of times, and (c) retry delays follow exponential backoff (capture call timestamps or mock sleep and verify delay progression). Also assert that exhausting max retries does raise the expected error rather than silently returning empty data.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The idempotency guarantee depends on how checkpoint state and PostgreSQL writes interact across two pipeline runs. The bug (duplication) only manifests when a second run re-processes rows that were already committed — which requires real persistent state. MOCK-HIDE: If PostgreSQL is mocked, an in-memory mock will typically not enforce unique constraints or transactional semantics, so a duplicating implementation would pass a mock-based test. A real test PostgreSQL instance (via Docker or a local PG server) is needed to surface actual duplication. REAL-INFRA: A local test DB is sufficient — no real production DB or network call is required, so this stays at Integration rather than System. The pipeline does not need to be restarted as a separate OS process; simulating mid-batch interruption by raising an exception or truncating the batch in the first call, then calling again, is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Spin up a test PostgreSQL database (e.g., via pytest-postgresql or a Docker fixture). Load a batch of rows. Run the pipeline up to a mid-batch checkpoint, then simulate a failure (e.g., raise an exception after N rows are committed). Run the pipeline again from the beginning with the same input. Query the PostgreSQL table and assert that each row appears exactly once — no duplicates on any natural key or pipeline-assigned ID. Also assert the total row count equals the number of valid input rows, not a multiple of it.
